title: Order of Friars Minor

infobox.value: On 4 October 1897, the Order of Discalced Friars Minor, Order of Observant Friars Minor, Order of Friars Minor Recollect, the Order of Reformed Friars Minor, et al., were merged into a single religious order named the Order of Friars Minor
Mendicant Order Institute of Consecrated Life - Men[[1]](#cite_note-catholic-hierarchy.org-1)
General Curia Via di S. Maria Mediatrice, 2500165 Roma, Italia[[1]](#cite_note-catholic-hierarchy.org-1)
Latin**:** *Pax et bonum* English**:**  *Peace and the good*
Preaching, missionary, educational, parochial, charitable works
[Secular Franciscan Order](/wiki/Secular_Franciscan_Order "Secular Franciscan Order") (1221) [Third Order of Saint Francis](/wiki/Third_Order_of_Saint_Francis "Third Order of Saint Francis") (1447)

[Order of Friars Minor Conventual](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Conventual "Order of Friars Minor Conventual") (1209) [Order of Friars Minor Capuchin](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Capuchin "Order of Friars Minor Capuchin") (1520)
*Pax et bonum* English**:**  *Peace and the good* |

(commonly called the [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans"), the **Franciscan Order**, or the **Seraphic Order**;[[2]](#cite_note-2) [postnominal](/wiki/Post-nominal_letters "Post-nominal letters") [abbreviation](/wiki/Abbreviation "Abbreviation") **OFM**) is a [mendicant](/wiki/Mendicant_orders "Mendicant orders") [Catholic religious order](/wiki/Catholic_religious_order "Catholic religious order"), founded in 1209 by [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi"). The order adheres to the teachings and spiritual disciplines of the founder and of his main associates and followers, such as [Clare of Assisi](/wiki/Clare_of_Assisi "Clare of Assisi"), [Anthony of Padua](/wiki/Anthony_of_Padua "Anthony of Padua"), and [Elizabeth of Hungary](/wiki/Elizabeth_of_Hungary "Elizabeth of Hungary"), among many others. The Order of Friars Minor is the largest of the contemporary [First Orders](/wiki/Religious_institute#Categorization "Religious institute") within the Franciscan movement.

branch (postnominal abbreviation OFM Obs.), is one of the three Franciscan [First Orders](/wiki/Religious_institute#Categorization "Religious institute") within the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), the others being the [Capuchins](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Capuchin "Order of Friars Minor Capuchin") (postnominal abbreviation OFM Cap.) and [Conventuals](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Conventual "Order of Friars Minor Conventual") (postnominal abbreviation OFM Conv). The Order of Friars Minor, in its current form, is the result of an amalgamation of several smaller Franciscan orders (e.g. [Alcantarines](/wiki/Discalced "Discalced"), [Recollects](/wiki/Recollects "Recollects"), [Reformanti](/w/index.php?title=Reformanti&action=edit&redlink=1 "Reformanti (page does not exist)"), etc.), completed in 1897 by [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII").[[5]](#cite_note-CatholicEncyclopediaOrderofFriarsMinor-5) The Capuchin and Conventual remain distinct [religious institutes](/wiki/Religious_institute "Religious institute") within the Catholic Church, observing the [Rule of Saint Francis](/wiki/Rule_of_Saint_Francis "Rule of Saint Francis") with different emphases. Franciscans are sometimes referred to as **minorites** or **greyfriars** because of their [habit](/wiki/Religious_habit "Religious habit"). In [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") they are known as **[Bernardines](/wiki/Bernardines_(Franciscans) "Bernardines (Franciscans)")**, after [Bernardino of Siena](/wiki/Bernardino_of_Siena "Bernardino of Siena"), although the term elsewhere refers rather to [Cistercians](/wiki/Cistercians "Cistercians").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Mendicant Catholic religious order

This article is about primarily the Observant branch of the Friars Minor, formed in 1897. For the other branches within the movement, see [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans").

[Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi"), founder of the Order of Friars Minor; oldest known portrait in existence of the saint, dating back to St. Francis' retreat to [Subiaco](/wiki/Subiaco,_Lazio "Subiaco, Lazio") (1223–1224)

The **Order of Friars Minor** (commonly called the [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans"), the **Franciscan Order**, or the **Seraphic Order**;[[2]](#cite_note-2) [postnominal](/wiki/Post-nominal_letters "Post-nominal letters") [abbreviation](/wiki/Abbreviation "Abbreviation") **OFM**) is a [mendicant](/wiki/Mendicant_orders "Mendicant orders") [Catholic religious order](/wiki/Catholic_religious_order "Catholic religious order"), founded in 1209 by [Francis of Assisi](/wiki/Francis_of_Assisi "Francis of Assisi"). The order adheres to the teachings and spiritual disciplines of the founder and of his main associates and followers, such as [Clare of Assisi](/wiki/Clare_of_Assisi "Clare of Assisi"), [Anthony of Padua](/wiki/Anthony_of_Padua "Anthony of Padua"), and [Elizabeth of Hungary](/wiki/Elizabeth_of_Hungary "Elizabeth of Hungary"), among many others. The Order of Friars Minor is the largest of the contemporary [First Orders](/wiki/Religious_institute#Categorization "Religious institute") within the Franciscan movement.

Francis began preaching around 1207 and traveled to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") to seek approval of his order from [Pope Innocent III](/wiki/Pope_Innocent_III "Pope Innocent III") in 1209. The original [Rule of Saint Francis](/wiki/Rule_of_Saint_Francis "Rule of Saint Francis") approved by the pope disallowed ownership of property, requiring members of the order to beg for food while preaching. The austerity was meant to emulate the life and ministry of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ"). Franciscans traveled and preached in the streets, while boarding in church properties. The extreme poverty required of members was relaxed in the final revision of the Rule in 1223. The degree of observance required of members remained a major source of conflict within the order, resulting in numerous secessions.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The Order of Friars Minor, previously known as the **Observant** branch (postnominal abbreviation OFM Obs.), is one of the three Franciscan [First Orders](/wiki/Religious_institute#Categorization "Religious institute") within the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), the others being the [Capuchins](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Capuchin "Order of Friars Minor Capuchin") (postnominal abbreviation OFM Cap.) and [Conventuals](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Conventual "Order of Friars Minor Conventual") (postnominal abbreviation OFM Conv). The Order of Friars Minor, in its current form, is the result of an amalgamation of several smaller Franciscan orders (e.g. [Alcantarines](/wiki/Discalced "Discalced"), [Recollects](/wiki/Recollects "Recollects"), [Reformanti](/w/index.php?title=Reformanti&action=edit&redlink=1 "Reformanti (page does not exist)"), etc.), completed in 1897 by [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII").[[5]](#cite_note-CatholicEncyclopediaOrderofFriarsMinor-5) The Capuchin and Conventual remain distinct [religious institutes](/wiki/Religious_institute "Religious institute") within the Catholic Church, observing the [Rule of Saint Francis](/wiki/Rule_of_Saint_Francis "Rule of Saint Francis") with different emphases. Franciscans are sometimes referred to as **minorites** or **greyfriars** because of their [habit](/wiki/Religious_habit "Religious habit"). In [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") they are known as **[Bernardines](/wiki/Bernardines_(Franciscans) "Bernardines (Franciscans)")**, after [Bernardino of Siena](/wiki/Bernardino_of_Siena "Bernardino of Siena"), although the term elsewhere refers rather to [Cistercians](/wiki/Cistercians "Cistercians").

## Name and demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Order_of_Friars_Minor&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name and demographics")]

See also: [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans") and [Franciscan spirituality in Protestantism](/wiki/Franciscan_spirituality_in_Protestantism "Franciscan spirituality in Protestantism")

The "Order of Friars Minor" are commonly called simply the "**Franciscans**". This Order is a [mendicant](/wiki/Mendicant_orders "Mendicant orders") [religious order](/wiki/Religious_order "Religious order") of men that traces its origin to Francis of Assisi.[[6]](#cite_note-Fordham-6) Their official [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") name is the *Ordo Fratrum Minorum*[[7]](#cite_note-ceofm-7) Which is the name Francis gave his brotherhood. Having been born among the minorum (serfs, second class citizens), before his conversion, he aspired to move up the social ladder to the maiorum (nobles, first class citizens). After a life of conversion, the name of his brotherhood (Order of Second-Class Brothers) indicates his coming to an appreciation of his social condition on behalf of those who have no class or citizenship in society.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The modern organization of the Friars Minor comprises several separate families or groups, each considered a religious order in its own right under its own Minister General and particular type of governance. They all live according to a body of regulations known as the Rule of St Francis.[[6]](#cite_note-Fordham-6) These are:

### The Order of Friars Minor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Order_of_Friars_Minor&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The Order of Friars Minor")]

The Order of Friars Minor, known as the "Observants", most commonly simply called **Franciscan friars**,[[6]](#cite_note-Fordham-6) official name: "Friars Minor" (OFM).[[9]](#cite_note-cefran-9) According to the 2013 *[Annuario Pontificio](/wiki/Annuario_Pontificio "Annuario Pontificio"),* the OFM has 2,212 communities; 14,123 members; 9,735 priests[[10]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-10)

### Order of Friars Minor Capuchin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Order_of_Friars_Minor&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Order of Friars Minor Capuchin")]

The [Order of Friars Minor Capuchin](/wiki/Order_of_Friars_Minor_Capuchin "Order of Friars Minor Capuchin") or simply **Capuchins**,[[6]](#cite_note-Fordham-6) official name: "Friars Minor Capuchin" (OFM Cap).[[9]](#cite_note-cefran-9) it has 1,633 communities; 10,786 members; 7,057 priests[[10]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-10)

### Conventual Franciscans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Order_of_Friars_Minor&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Conventual Franciscans")]

The [Conventual Franciscans](/wiki/Conventual_Franciscans "Conventual Franciscans") or **Minorites**,[[6]](#cite_note-Fordham-6) official name: "Friars Minor Conventual" (OFM Conv).[[9]](#cite_note-cefran-9) It has 667 communities; 4,289 members; 2,921 priests[[10]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Order_of_Friars_Minor&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Franciscans § History](/wiki/Franciscans#History "Franciscans")

### Beginnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Order_of_Friars_Minor&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Beginnings")]

A sermon on [Mt 10:9](/wiki/Matthew_10 "Matthew 10") which Francis heard in 1209 made such an impression on him that he decided to fully devote himself to a life of apostolic poverty. Clad in a rough garment, barefoot, and, after the [Evangelical](/wiki/Evangelism "Evangelism") precept, without staff or scrip, he began to preach repentance.[[11]](#cite_note-cefa-11)

The mendicant orders had long been exempt from the jurisdiction of the bishop, and enjoyed (as distinguished from the secular clergy) unrestricted freedom to preach and hear confessions in the churches connected with their monasteries. This had led to endless friction and open quarrels between the two divisions of the clergy. This question was definitively settled by the [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent").[[5]](#cite_note-CatholicEncyclopediaOrderofFriarsMinor-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Franciscan_monastery_Lopud.JPG/250px-Franciscan_monastery_Lopud.JPG)](/wiki/File:Franciscan_monastery_Lopud.JPG)

Franciscan convent at Lopud in [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia")

### Separate congregations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Order_of_Friars_Minor&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Separate congregations")]

Amid numerous dissensions in the 14th century, a number of separate observances sprang up, almost like sects (to say nothing of the heretical parties of the [Beghards](/wiki/Beghards "Beghards") and [Fraticelli](/wiki/Fraticelli "Fraticelli")), some of which developed within the order on both hermit and cenobitic principles. They all operated generally under the authority of the [Minister General](/wiki/Minister_General "Minister General") of the Order, a member of the Observant branch, but were allowed their distinct practices. They included:

* The Clareni or Clarenini, an association of hermits established on the river Clareno in the march of [Ancona](/wiki/Ancona "Ancona") by [Angelo da Clareno](/wiki/Angelo_da_Clareno "Angelo da Clareno") after the suppression of the Franciscan Celestines by Boniface VIII. Like several other smaller congregations, it was obliged in 1568 under [Pope Pius V](/wiki/Pope_Pius_V "Pope Pius V") to unite with the general body of Observantists.
* The quasi-Observantist brothers living under the rule of the Conventual ministers (Martinianists or "Observantes sub ministris"), such as the Colletans--formed under the guidance of [Colette of Corbie](/wiki/Colette_of_Corbie "Colette of Corbie") and led by Boniface de Ceva in their reform attempts principally in France and Germany;
* The reformed congregation founded in 1426 by the Spaniard Philip de Berbegal and distinguished by the special importance they attached to the little hood (*cappuciola*);
* The Neutri, a group of reformers originating about 1463 in Italy, who tried to take a middle ground between the Conventuals and Observantists, but refused to obey the heads of either, until they were compelled by the pope to affiliate with the regular Observantists, or with those of the Common Life;
* The Caperolani, a congregation founded about 1470 in North Italy by [Pietro Caperolo](/wiki/Pietro_Caperolo "Pietro Caperolo"), but dissolved on the death of its founder in 1481;
* The Amadist friars, established by [Amadeus of Portugal](/wiki/Amadeus_of_Portugal "Amadeus of Portugal") in 1472, the same year that he was selected to serve as the [confessor](/wiki/Confessor "Confessor") to the pope. The [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") entrusted him with the Church of [San Pietro in Minotorio](/w/index.php?title=San_Pietro_in_Minotorio&action=edit&redlink=1 "San Pietro in Minotorio (page does not exist)") to serve as the [motherhouse](/wiki/Motherhouse "Motherhouse") of his growing reform movement. They existed until 1568, when they were merged into the Observant branch of the Order.

### Rule on property

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Order_of_Friars_Minor&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Rule on property")]

A difference of opinion developed in the community concerning the interpretation of the rule regarding property. The Observants held to a strict interpretation that the friars may not hold any property either individually nor communally. The literal and unconditional observance of this was rendered impracticable by the great expansion of the order, its pursuit of learning, and the accumulated property of the large cloisters in the towns. Regulations were drafted by which all alms donated were held by custodians appointed by the Holy See, who would make distributions upon request. It was John XXII who had introduced Conventualism in the sense of community of goods, income, and property as in other religious orders, in contradiction to Observantism or the strict observance of the rule. [Pope Martin V](/wiki/Pope_Martin_V "Pope Martin V"), in the Brief *Ad statum* of 23 August 1430, allowed the Conventuals to hold property like all other orders.[[5]](#cite_note-CatholicEncyclopediaOrderofFriarsMinor-5)

### Attempted union between branches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Order_of_Friars_Minor&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Attempted union between branches")]

Projects for a union between the two main branches of the order were put forth not only by the Council of Constance but by several popes, without any positive result. By direction of [Pope Martin V](/wiki/Pope_Martin_V "Pope Martin V"), [John of Capistrano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Capistrano "Giovanni da Capistrano") drew up statutes which were to serve as a basis for reunion, and they were actually accepted by a general chapter at Assisi in 1430; but the majority of the Conventual houses refused to agree to them, and they remained without effect.

Equally unsuccessful were the attempts of the Franciscan [Pope Sixtus IV](/wiki/Pope_Sixtus_IV "Pope Sixtus IV"), who bestowed a vast number of privileges on both original mendicant orders, but by this very fact lost the favor of the Observants and failed in his plans for reunion. [Julius II](/wiki/Pope_Julius_II "Pope Julius II") succeeded in doing away with some of the smaller branches, but left the division of the two great parties untouched. This division was finally legalized by [Leo X](/wiki/Pope_Leo_X "Pope Leo X"), after a general chapter held in Rome in 1517, in connection with the reform movement of the [Fifth Lateran Council](/wiki/Fifth_Council_of_the_Lateran "Fifth Council of the Lateran"), had once more declared the impossibility of reunion. Leo X summoned on 11 July 1516 a general chapter to meet at Rome on the feast of Pentecost 31 May 1517. This chapter suppressed all the reformed congregations and annexed them to the Observants; it then declared the Observants an independent order, and separated them completely from the Conventuals.[[5]](#cite_note-CatholicEncyclopediaOrderofFriarsMinor-5) The less strict principles of the Conventuals, permitting the possession of real estate and the enjoyment of fixed revenues, were recognized as tolerable, while the Observants, in contrast to this *usus moderatus*, were held strictly to their own *usus arctus* or *pauper*.

### Unification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Order_of_Friars_Minor&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Unification")]

All of the groups that followed the Franciscan Rule literally were united to the Observants, and the right to elect the [Minister General of the Order](/wiki/Minister_General_(Franciscan) "Minister General (Franciscan)"), together with the seal of the order, was given to the group united under the Observants. This grouping, since it adhered more closely to the rule of the founder, was allowed to claim a certain superiority over the Conventuals. The Observant general (elected now for six years, not for life) inherited the title of "Minister-General of the Whole Order of St. Francis" and was granted the right to confirm the choice of a head for the Conventuals, who was known as "Master-General of the Friars Minor Conventual"—although this privilege never became practically operative.

In 1875, the [Kulturkampf](/wiki/Kulturkampf "Kulturkampf") expelled the majority of the German Franciscans, most of whom settled in North America.[[5]](#cite_note-CatholicEncyclopediaOrderofFriarsMinor-5)

## The habit and the French name Cordeliers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Order_of_Friars_Minor&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: The habit and the French name Cordeliers")]

The habit has been gradually changed in colour and certain other details. Its colour, which was at first grey or a medium brown, is now a dark brown. The dress, which consists of a loose-sleeved gown, is confined by a white cord, from which is hung, since the fifteenth century, the [Seraphic Rosary](/wiki/Franciscan_Crown "Franciscan Crown") with its seven decades. Sandals are substituted for shoes. Around the neck and over the shoulders hangs the cowl.[[5]](#cite_note-CatholicEncyclopediaOrderofFriarsMinor-5)

The habit of referring to the Francisans as Cordeliers in France is said to date back to the [Seventh Crusade](/wiki/Seventh_Crusade "Seventh Crusade"), when [Louis IX](/wiki/Louis_IX_of_France "Louis IX of France") asked who the particularly zealous monks pursuing [Saracens](/wiki/Saracen "Saracen") were, and was told they were *"de cordes liés"*. Upon the crusaders return to France, the name became part of the language.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
